L1.area: REST API endpoint handling and OAuth token management

L2.description: An authorization bypass vulnerability exists where OAuth access tokens are not validated against their granted scopes when processing API requests. The token response mechanism reports all available scopes regardless of what was actually granted, and API endpoint handlers only verify that a user is authenticated and has sufficient user-level privileges, without checking that the access token was authorized for the requested action. This allows an attacker who obtains a token with limited scope to perform privileged operations including administrative actions on the server.

L3.area: OAuth bearer token response customization and API controller request authorization
L3.description: An authorization bypass vulnerability exists in a Mastodon-compatible social media platform where OAuth access tokens are not validated against their granted scopes when processing API requests. The bearer token response hardcodes all available permission scopes in the token metadata regardless of what was actually granted to the OAuth application. Additionally, API endpoint handlers throughout the application only verify that a user is authenticated and has the appropriate user role (e.g., admin status), without checking whether the access token was authorized for the specific scope required by the operation. This allows an attacker who obtains a read-only access token to perform write operations, follow actions, and even administrative configuration changes on the instance.

ground_truth.reason: API endpoints only checked user authentication and role (e.g., admin status) without validating that the OAuth access token had the required scopes, and the bearer token response hardcoded all scopes regardless of what was actually granted. This allowed attackers to use a read-only access token to perform write, follow, and administrative operations.
